behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed—unchanged files are not scanned.
behaviors.justification: The real failure boundary is the GitHub API response structure and file filtering integration. A Unit test with mocks could miss: (1) actual GitHub API field names/types that differ from mock assumptions, (2) pagination edge cases when PR changes many files, (3) file renames/deletes returning unexpected structures. Integration test with real GitHub API (or test repository with real PR context) is needed to catch schema mismatches that mocks hide. MOCK-MISS: yes—mocks are often oversimplified. REAL-INFRA: yes—real GitHub API call is the boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Call real GitHub API or use test GitHub repository with a test PR containing known changed files. Verify that only the actual changed files from the PR diff are passed to the linter, not all repository files. Include edge cases: renamed files, binary files, deletions.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly requires findings to appear 'in the posted PR comment'—the posted artifact itself is the test target. Unit tests verify grouping logic works; Integration tests verify the comment is formatted correctly; but System test is needed to verify the actual comment posted to GitHub contains grouped findings. Real failure: comment posts but GitHub API truncates/rejects/reformats it; or network call fails. MOCK-HIDE: yes—mocking the API call would hide posting failures. REAL-INFRA: yes—real GitHub API call to post and verify the comment exists on the PR.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test PR with known findings from the linter. Run the pipeline (in GitHub Actions or equivalent environment), and verify that the actual posted PR comment groups findings by severity, with all findings present and formatted correctly. Validate the comment appears on the PR.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout.
behaviors.justification: Real failure is timing/performance in the GitHub Actions environment. Unit tests with 200 items would catch O(n²) logic bugs but miss: (1) actual linter subprocess overhead (real static analysis tools are slow), (2) GitHub Actions environment overhead and resource constraints, (3) file I/O patterns on real filesystem, (4) network latency for API calls. Integration test with real linter on 200 files would help but wouldn't capture Actions-specific overhead. REAL-INFRA: yes—subprocess performance, filesystem I/O, and Actions environment constraints all matter.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test PR with exactly 200 changed files of realistic size and content. Run the linter step in a GitHub Actions environment and measure total runtime from start to posting comment. Verify it completes well under 10 minutes (e.g., under 5 minutes to ensure headroom).

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted.
behaviors.justification: Real failure has two parts: (1) exception handling when linter crashes—could be tested at Integration with real linter subprocess, (2) results posted despite crash—requires real GitHub API call. The behavior says 'results posted', meaning we must verify the actual API call succeeds and the comment appears on the PR, not just that code attempted to call it. MOCK-MISS: yes—mocking the GitHub API would hide failures in the actual posting flow after a crash. REAL-INFRA: yes—real subprocess crash behavior and real API call both matter.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test PR with one malformed file that crashes the real linter, surrounded by valid files. Run the action in GitHub Actions environment. Verify: (1) the linter crashes on the malformed file, (2) remaining files are still analyzed with results generated, (3) a comment is successfully posted to the PR with results from the non-crashed files.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output.
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is a pure logic problem: given a list of findings with overlapping line/rule combinations, remove duplicates. Real failure is a logic bug (wrong comparison, wrong removal strategy). Unit test with synthetic findings can thoroughly test this. While Integration test with real linter output would add confidence (verifies real linter format works), the logic itself is deterministic and doesn't depend on linter internals. MOCK-HIDE: no—deduplication logic is independent of where findings come from. LLM-DEP: no—fully deterministic.
behaviors.test_description: Create a list of synthetic findings where multiple findings share the same line number and rule ID. Pass through the deduplication function and verify: (1) exact duplicates are removed, (2) findings with different rules on same line are kept, (3) findings with different lines are kept, (4) order doesn't affect deduplication. Test edge cases like empty list, single finding, all duplicates.
